posts.title: Why I Only Shoot With My Phone
posts.excerpt: There's a theory that constraints breed creativity. I've been testing it for four years with nothing but the camera in my pocket.
posts.body:
There's a theory that constraints breed creativity. I've been testing it for four years with nothing but the camera in my pocket.

When I first started photographing seriously, I had a DSLR. It was a good camera — technically capable, undeniably impressive. But I noticed something: people saw it coming. The moment I raised it, expressions changed. The street shifted. The light on a woman's face at Makola market would flatten the instant she noticed the lens.

The phone changed everything. It's invisible. People look at their own phones all day; one more person staring at a small rectangle is unremarkable. It gave me access to moments that the DSLR kept locked behind a wall of self-consciousness.

The technical limitations forced me to think harder about light. You can't zoom your way to a good photograph on a phone — you have to move, get closer, wait. The discipline became part of the practice.

posts.title: Stillness in Jamestown
posts.excerpt: The photograph almost didn't happen. I was leaving when the light shifted and I turned back.
posts.body:
The photograph almost didn't happen. I was leaving when the light shifted and I turned back.

I'd been in Jamestown for the afternoon — one of Accra's oldest neighbourhoods, where fishing boats still launch from the beach at dawn and the colonial-era lighthouse looks out over a coastline that hasn't changed in decades.

I was walking toward the main road when the clouds broke at a particular angle. The light didn't announce itself; it just arrived. A man was sitting alone on a low wall, his back to me, looking at nothing in particular. The shadow line crossed him at exactly the right place.

I had six seconds, maybe seven, before the cloud moved back. I got three frames. Only one worked.

That print — Stillness in Jamestown — is now in the shop. It's an edition of 15. There will never be more than 15.

posts.title: On Editions and Why They Matter
posts.excerpt: Limiting editions is a promise. Here's what that promise actually means.
posts.body:
Limiting editions is a promise. Here's what that promise actually means.

When I say a print is an edition of 15, I mean exactly 15 will ever be produced — signed, numbered, certified. Not 15 this year, not 15 and then a reprint. 15. When they're gone, that's it.

This matters for two reasons. The first is artistic: scarcity forces a kind of accountability. If I commit to only 15 of something, I have to be sure that something deserves to exist in the world. It can't be a filler piece. The discipline sharpens the edit.

The second reason is for collectors. When you buy a print, you're not buying a JPEG someone else can reproduce infinitely. You're buying a specific, numbered object — physical proof of a moment, certified by my signature and a document that travels with it. The certificate isn't bureaucracy; it's a record of what you own and why it's real.
